project:
[color=12edd4]By Jeffrey S. with help from Alex F, Ella F, Camille K. and our lovely community.[/color])

[color=12edd4]Font: DamienG[/color]

A Spirit Portrait
by Adelaide D.

[color=e139ff]Adelaide:[/color]

[color=ff9838]When you're making a painting, what exactly is your thought process? Do you have an idea of what you want to create beforehand or do you just go into it blindly?[/color]

[color=678cff]Ivokyuftaf6666:[/color]

[color=ff9838]At the beginning I go randomly and I accept every random suggestion made by the software, as long as I like it overall.

But over time I build a concept of what else I want to have in my picture and based on this I choose the rest of the brushes on my own.[/color]

[color=678cff]Ivokyuftaf6666:[/color]

[color=ff9838]Also, sometimes if I notice a way to do this, I might start a painting with the idea to create something symmetric.  And when I decide that I want to draw something specific, I even have strategies of how to draw some of the things.[/color]
 
[color=e139ff]Adelaide:[/color]

[color=ff9838]Do you work with any other art forms? Like drawing, or photography?[/color]

[color=678cff]Ivokyuftaf6666:[/color]

[color=ff9838]Over the years I have created many ceramics works. I have made some animations on a website and I have drawn many pictures in MS Paint.[/color]

[color=e139ff]Adelaide:[/color]

[color=ff9838]What is one of your favorite places to be and why?[/color]

[color=678cff]Ivokyuftaf6666:[/color]

[color=ff9838]In the offline part of the life [color=ffffff]([/color] the seaside beach and other places in nature, because of the peaceful and refreshing environment.[/color]

[color=ff9838]In the online part of the life [color=ffffff])[/color] No Paint, Crunchyroll and ASDF, because of the interesting and pleasant activities and the awesome people. Any time, I feel wonderfully alone at home.[/color]

A Spirit Portrait
by Alex F.

[color=8E150B]Alex:[/color]

[color=B4AB90]I notice these almost archetypal, folk characters explode from your pixels! '[color=ffffff]^=[/color]mushroom cloud[color=ffffff]=^[/color]' and '[color=ffffff]$$[/color]The Council[color=ffffff]$$[/color]' have their own unique, deeply affective faces. What draws you to exporting these expressions?[/color]

[color=003DA2]Adelaide:[/color]

[color=B4AB90]I really just go with whatever I think feels right at the time, until some tangible idea or structure reveals itself. A face is arguably the most recognizable figure to exist and, because of my process... or lack thereof, a recurring theme within my paintings.[/color]

[color=8E150B]Alex:[/color]

[color=B4AB90]Does working with one set of tools or within a new software environment change how you work with other tools, in other environments? Does your desire to draw a face increase or decrease depending on the tools, for example?[/color]

[color=003DA2]Adelaide:[/color]

[color=B4AB90]Are you asking if using software that has different limits impacts the way that I work in other areas? Like, from No Paint to pen, or from pen to Procreate? If you are, I feel like it doesn't really. That's actually something I've been consciously trying to get better at... transferring the[/color]

[color=003DA2]continued:[/color]

[color=B4AB90]way I work in one environment to a different one. 

Like, I feel like you can compare two of my digital paintings to one another and be able to tell that I made them. And two paintings I made in No Paint. But if you were to compare that painting to the digital painting you couldn't tell. Does that make sense?[/color]

[color=8E150B]Alex:[/color]

[color=B4AB90]So it's important for you to close the stylistic distance between say a No Paint painting, a Procreate painting, and a drawing?[/color]

[color=003DA2]Adelaide:[/color]

[color=B4AB90]Yeah, definitely. I feel like it's a really important step in honing my skills. It's much easier to spend time refining one general style than five different ones. That's not to say I want to do the exact same thing over and over and over again, but I do want to find more of a middle ground. Some kind of amalgamation of all those styles.[/color]
 
[color=8E150B]Alex:[/color]

[color=B4AB90]Are you satisfied with the ways you can share your work, be it on Discord or Instagram or anywhere else? Is sharing work with a community important for feeling like your work is complete?[/color]

[color=003DA2]Adelaide:[/color]

[color=B4AB90]I'm definitely satisfied with the communities I've been able to share with, but I'm always willing to find broader platforms to use, or places with more reach. Sharing my work is so, so important to me. Showing something to a community, or even just a few friends is one of the primary reasons why I create. Knowing that someone felt something because of a work I made is so incredible to me.

Haha can you tell I've given up on being professional? [/color]

A Spirit Portrait
by Alex F.

[color=005D36]Alex:[/color]

[color=b7abbf]What do you see when you look at the compositions of yours that are highly detailed on a pixel by pixel scale, like '[color=ffffff]^[/color]Eeeeeeeee[color=ffffff]^[/color]' and 'Timed'? What ways of thinking might they encourage for you, and how does this differ from your more [color=9482a0]Stamp[/color]-heavy works and [color=9482a0]Spin[/color] pieces?[/color]

[color=663E54]Wilsans:[/color]

[color=b7abbf]I see effort, somehow. It takes a lot of time to get it all pixelated like that, because of all the [color=9482a0]Quicksands[/color] you have to do to jumble it all up...making it to the end and [color=9482a0]Mirroring[/color] it into a bunch of pretty patterns and making the colors all vibrant is definitely something that helps me push through.

My Spin pieces and less detailed Mirror works all started as experiments, while my pixel art has much more of a routine to it.

But they still all have one thing in common, the color. Eyestrain is my thing for No Paint, and I'd say I do a pretty good job at it.[/color]

[color=663E54]continued:[/color]

[color=b7abbf]helps me push through.

My [color=9482a0]Spin[/color] pieces and less detailed [color=9482a0]Mirror[/color] works all started as experiments, while my pixel art has much more of a routine to it.

But they still all have one thing in common, the color. Eyestrain is my thing for No Paint, and I'd say I do a pretty good job at it.[/color]
 
[color=005D36]Alex:[/color]

[color=b7abbf]What do you think of the eyestrain phenomenon? Do these paintings strain your eyes when you're working on them, or reflecting on them as in this interview? And what has your relationship been to warning people of the potential for eyestrain? Seeing as it is it's own category separate from say, an epilepsy warning.[/color]

[color=005D36]continued:[/color]

[color=b7abbf]been to warning people of the potential for eyestrain? Seeing as it is its own category separate from, say, an epilepsy warning.[/color]

[color=663E54]Wilsans:[/color]

[color=b7abbf]They only hurt my eyes a teeny bit, I have a pretty good tolerance to it. When I first started I used to put spoiler/eyestrain warnings whenever I posted them, but no one has ever really said anything about it so I stopped. Of course if anyone does need me to put warnings back on them I will.

I remember posting old paintings and not really having them go anywhere, but then I posted one spin piece with a lot of contrast and people loved it. So I kept going, incorporating the bright colors in most of my nopaints since.[/color]

[color=663E54]continued:[/color]

[color=b7abbf]then I posted one [color=9482a0]Spin[/color] piece with a lot of [color=9482a0]Contrast[/color] and people loved it. So I kept going, incorporating the bright colors in most of my pictures in No Paint since.[/color]
 
[color=005D36]Alex:[/color]

[color=b7abbf]How important is sharing your work with others, and how does that further influence your process?[/color]

[color=663E54]Wilsans:[/color]

[color=b7abbf]It doesn't really influence anything. I don't make these pictures to show them to others, even though it is super sick to post them and have people tell me how cool they are. I do it all for me. It's a nice side hobby, cause there's so much you can do with such little control. [/color]

[color=663E54]continued:[/color]

[color=b7abbf]It's a nice side hobby, because there's so much you can do with such little control.

Also let me add that I love seeing what other people create with those controls, and I think I might've had some influence on other artists along the way.[/color]

[color=005D36]Alex:[/color]

[color=b7abbf]I have a feeling that's very true![/color]

A Spirit Portrait
by Alex F.

[color=50A359]Sean:[/color]

[color=989DB1]The hardest thing about choosing four paintings is thinking about things I can say about each one, and showing the wide gamut of styles I do with No Paint while making it palatable for people. I think we get into problems with some of my favorites personally.[/color]
 
[color=E25A12]Alex:[/color]

[color=989DB1]Why do you think we get into problems with some of the ones that might be your true favorites?[/color]

[color=50A359]Sean:[/color]

[color=989DB1]Mostly titling/thematic issues.[/color]

[color=E25A12]Alex:[/color]

[color=989DB1]You speak to desiring a perfect finished work, but I see you uniquely title each draft that paves the way towards that end. What is the importance of these drafts versus your finished paintings once you've moved on from them? [/color]

[color=50A359]Sean:[/color]

[color=989DB1]I mostly create work in progress pieces for when I make an important decision which heavily alters a painting, or if I want to share a draft to the No Paint Discord server. I find those drafts important because that is how I can remember the way I created my work.[color=E25A12]

[color=E25A12]Alex:[/color]

[color=989DB1]Are there paintings you've made in No Paint that you haven't shown anyone? How important is sharing a piece you're working on?[/color]

[color=50A359]Sean:[/color]

[color=989DB1]About a dozen to two dozen that are not works in progress? Most of them are from a time when I was not as confident with my No Paint abilities. Sharing/exporting paintings is very important to me because I feel good knowing that there are people who care about my work. When I don't feel good about a painting, I don't post it. Now, I just mess around the canvas to get something I like.[/color]

[color=E25A12]Alex:[/color]

[color=989DB1]How do you start a given project? And please take me through your process of titling.[/color]

[color=50A359]Sean:[/color]

[color=989DB1]In the last two months or so, I don't use the [color=737995]Reset[/color] button often. I paint over my completed works because generating ideas from a used canvas are easier than an uninspiring wall of color. Using old, completed paintings also allows me to spend less time creating a brand new background. In terms of how I title my work, I think about names way too long. The title is either meant to be descriptive, or stupid. You don't see the unusual names.[/color]

[color=50A359]continued:[/color]

[color=989DB1]unusual names in the paintings I'm showing here, but the names get pretty wild.[/color]
unused - generic - no button release (ew buzz)
